perturbation.original_text: 1.1 The Intellectual Property 1.1.1 Upon the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth, Party A hereby agrees to grant and Party B hereby agree to accept the right to use the intellectual property in PRC. The license under this Agreement is a non-exclusive, non-assignable and non-transferable license.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 The Intellectual Property 1.1.1 Upon the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth, Party A hereby agrees to grant and Party B hereby agree to accept the right to use the intellectual property in PRC. The license under this Agreement is an exclusive, assignable and transferable license.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the license from 'non-exclusive, non-assignable and non-transferable' to 'exclusive, assignable and transferable' directly contradicts the initial intention and changes the fundamental nature of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 168 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China addresses the enforceability of technology import and export contract provisions, including intellectual property licensing. By making the license 'exclusive, assignable and transferable' without specifying any restrictions or conditions, it potentially violates provisions relating to maintaining fair competition and protecting the legitimate rights and interests of third parties, thus contradicting the legal framework governing such agreements.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 'non-exclusive, non-assignable and non-transferable license', while the changed text specifies an 'exclusive, assignable and transferable license'. This directly contradicts the initial agreement regarding the nature of the license granted, fundamentally altering the rights and obligations of the parties involved. The Law Explanation indicates that this change, by making the license 'exclusive, assignable and transferable' without restrictions, potentially violates provisions relating to maintaining fair competition and protecting the legitimate rights and interests of third parties, which are concerns addressed by Article 168 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China. Although no scraped snippets are available, the perturbation explanation combined with the Law Explanation strongly suggests a legal contradiction because the alteration significantly shifts the power dynamic and scope of the license in a way that could undermine fair competition and third-party rights.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Party B shall protect and maintain the confidentiality of any and all confidential data and information acknowledged or received by Party B from Party A (collectively the "Confidential Information"). Upon termination of this Agreement, Party B shall return Confidential Information to Party A or destroy it itself and delete Confidential Information from any electronic devices and cease to use them as required by Party A. Party B shall not disclose, grant or transfer any Confidential Information to any third party and will not use the Confidential Information without Party A's written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Party B shall protect and maintain the confidentiality of any and all confidential data and information acknowledged or received by Party B from Party A (collectively the "Confidential Information"). Upon termination of this Agreement, Party B may retain Confidential Information to Party B's use and will not return Confidential Information to Party A or destroy it and delete Confidential Information from any electronic devices. Party B shall disclose, grant or transfer any Confidential Information to any third party without Party A's written consent only when requested by law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the conditions on Confidential Information, especially allowing it to be disclosed when 'requested by law' can be contradictory to the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Anti-Unfair Competition Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 9 of the Anti-Unfair Competition Law protects trade secrets. The modification allows Party B to disclose confidential information when 'requested by law', which could potentially be interpreted broadly and undermine the protection of Party A's trade secrets, thereby contradicting the intended protection of confidential information outlined in the contract and potentially violating the Anti-Unfair Competition Law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent to protect Confidential Information, potentially violating the Anti-Unfair Competition Law. The original text mandates that upon termination, Party B must return or destroy confidential information. The modified text, however, allows Party B to retain and disclose confidential information 'when requested by law.' This creates a loophole that could be exploited to undermine the protection of Party A's trade secrets, which is the essence of the Article 9, SECURED TRANSACTIONS, as extracted from the U.C.C. The broad exception could negate the initial confidentiality agreement, enabling disclosures that would not have been permissible under the original contract terms. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' where the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Settlement of Disputes: Both Parties shall strive to settle any dispute arising from the interpretation or performance through friendly consultation. In case no settlement can be reached through consultation within 30 days after one party ask for consultation, each party can submit such matter to China International Economic and Trade Arbitration Commission (the "CIETAC") in accordance with its rules, and the arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Chinese and shall take place in Beijing. The arbitration award shall be final and conclusive and binding upon the parties and shall be enforceable in accordance with its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Settlement of Disputes: Both Parties shall strive to settle any dispute arising from the interpretation or performance through friendly consultation. In case no settlement can be reached through consultation within 30 days after one party ask for consultation, each party can submit such matter to a court in any country according to the location of the dispute, and the legal proceedings shall be conducted in any language according to the selected court. The court decision shall be reviewed de novo.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the dispute resolution from CIETAC arbitration to court proceedings in any country and allowing 'de novo' review contradicts the standard arbitration agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arbitration Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 9 of the Arbitration Law stipulates that a valid arbitration agreement precludes parties from initiating litigation in court regarding the same dispute. By allowing either party to submit the dispute to a court in any country and permitting a 'de novo' review, the modified text directly contradicts the principle of binding arbitration, which aims for a final and conclusive resolution outside of court. This undermines the agreed-upon arbitration process and violates the provisions of the Arbitration Law regarding the exclusive jurisdiction of arbitration tribunals when a valid arbitration agreement exists.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies dispute resolution through CIETAC arbitration, whereas the changed text allows submission to a court in any country with 'de novo' review. This directly contradicts the purpose of an arbitration agreement, which aims for a final and conclusive resolution outside of court, as stated in the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation's assertion that Article 9 of the Arbitration Law stipulates a valid arbitration agreement precludes litigation regarding the same dispute supports the claim that the modified text creates a contradiction, as the original text intended to use arbitration.
